turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito has the opinion of the court today in 16-6795, Ayestas v. Davis.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Petitioner Carlos Ayestas was convicted of murder in a State Court in Texas and was sentenced to death. After unsuccessfully seeking relief in the State Courts, he filed a habeas petition in Federal Court. He argued that his trial counsel rendered ineffective assistance by presenting insufficient mitigation evidence at the sentencing phase of his trial. To help obtain habeas relief, petitioner requested federal funds to investigate the mitigation evidence that he believes his state trial and post-conviction counsels should have uncovered. A federal statute, 18 U.S.C. §3599(f), permits District Courts to authorize such funds if the applicant demonstrates that the investigative services are ?reasonably? necessary for the representation of the defendant. The District Court denied the funding request and the Fifth Circuit affirmed. Both of those courts required petitioner to show that he had "a substantial" need for the requested services and both courts also denied habeas relief on the merits. We granted certiorari to review the lower court's interpretation of the funding statute and we now vacate the Fifth Circuit's judgment. We have jurisdiction to review the District Court's funding decision because it was a judicial, not an administrative act, and we hold that the lower courts misinterpreted the statute. The Fifth Circuit's interpretation, the substantial need test sets a more onerous burden than Section 3599 demands which refers to funding that is reasonably necessary. So the Fifth Circuit's burden is a little bit greater than the one that the statute prescribes. We therefore remand for further proceedings. The opinion of the court is unanimous. Justice Sotomayor has filed the concurring opinion, in which Justice Ginsburg has joined.
